stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z + ([[b]])}{z^{2} + ([[c]])z + ([[d]])} \), whose denominator has simple roots at \( z = [[p]] \) and \( z = [[q]] \). Let \( C \) be the positively oriented circle \( |z| = [[R]] \). Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \), counting only the pole that actually lies inside \( C \).
solution: The denominator factors as \( (z-([[p]]))(z-([[q]])) \), so the poles are \( z=[[p]] \) and \( z=[[q]] \). Since \( |[[p]]| < [[R]] < |[[q]]| \), only \( z=[[p]] \) lies inside \( C \). By the residue theorem, \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i\operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]}f = 2\pi i\cdot\dfrac{([[a]])([[p]])+([[b]])}{([[p]])-([[q]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums the residues at both roots, but \( z=[[q]] \) lies outside \( |z|=[[R]] \) and must be excluded.
This uses only the residue at \( z=[[q]] \), which is not even enclosed by \( C \).
The integral is not \( 0 \); the pole at \( z=[[p]] \) is genuinely enclosed by \( C \).
This is off by \( 2\pi i \) from the correct value.

This has the wrong sign (contour orientation reversed).

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{[[c1]]}{z - ([[p]])} + \dfrac{[[c2]]}{z - ([[q]])} \), which has simple poles at \( z = [[p]] \) and \( z = [[q]] \) with residues \( [[c1]] \) and \( [[c2]] \) respectively. Let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing both poles. Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: By the residue theorem, \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i\sum\operatorname{Res} \). Reading the residues directly from the partial-fraction form, the sum is \( [[c1]] + [[c2]] \), so the integral is \( 2\pi i([[c1]]+[[c2]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the residue at \( z=[[p]] \); the theorem sums the residues at all enclosed poles.

stem: Let \( f(z) = \dfrac{([[a]])z^{2} + ([[b]])z + ([[c]])}{(z-([[p]]))(z-([[q]]))(z-([[r]]))} \), which has simple poles at \( z = [[p]], [[q]], [[r]] \). Let \( C \) be a positively oriented simple closed contour enclosing all three poles. Using the residue theorem, compute \( \oint_{C} f(z)\,dz \).
solution: Summing the residues at all three simple poles, the terms involving \( b,c \) and the individual pole locations cancel, leaving the sum of residues equal to the leading coefficient \( [[a]] \) (this always happens when the denominator's degree exceeds the numerator's by exactly one). So \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i([[a]]) = [[answer]] \).

solution: Only \( z=[[p]] \) lies inside \( |z|=[[R]] \) (since \( |[[q]]| > [[R]] \)). For a double pole, \( \operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]}f = \dfrac{d}{dz}\!\left[\dfrac{([[a]])z^{2}+([[b]])z+([[c]])}{z-([[q]])}\right]_{z=[[p]]} \), so \( \oint_C f\,dz = 2\pi i\operatorname{Res}_{z=[[p]]}f = [[answer]] \).
